title: Journal prompts as practice
subtitle: The tight-question, free-write, no-editing method.
excerpt: A reference article for the journal prompt practices in the library. Covers the free-write style Rebecca uses across The Money Journal, why prompts are tight specific questions and not blank pages, and how to work a prompt set in fifteen minutes.
sourceNotes: The tight-question free-write style is the one Rebecca J Page uses across The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (2025), which holds 84 daily prompts. The wider free-write method has multiple lineages, most prominently Julia Cameron's morning pages (The Artist's Way, 1992) and Peter Elbow's freewriting (Writing Without Teachers, 1973); Rebecca's specific version uses a timer and a question rather than open pages.

practice.whenToUse: Read once before working any of the journal prompt sets in the library.

glossaryTerms.definition: Writing for a fixed period without stopping, editing, or re-reading. The pen keeps moving even when the page goes blank.
A journal prompt that names one specific thing to write about. "When did 'we never get ahead' first become true for my family?" rather than "How do you feel about money?"

content.text: Journal prompts in the library are tight specific questions, not blank pages. Each prompt names one thing to write about. The user writes for five minutes without stopping, doesn't re-read or edit, and moves on. A prompt set holds two to four prompts that walk a single feeling through; fifteen minutes for a full set is typical.

Set a timer for five minutes before each prompt. Read the prompt once. Start writing. Don't stop, don't re-read, don't edit. If the page goes blank, keep the pen moving — write "I don't know what to write" until something else surfaces. When the timer ends, close the notebook. Move to the next prompt or stop for the day.
The timer matters. It tells the body that the writing has a stop point, and that loosens the writing. Without it, the page becomes another open-ended task and the writing stays tight.

The no-editing rule matters too. Re-reading interrupts the surfacing. The insight in a free-write usually lands two or three days after the writing, not at the end of the page. Close the notebook; come back to it later if at all.
Why tight questions, not blank pages

A blank-page prompt ("How do you feel about money?") gives the head too much room to perform. A tight question ("When did 'we never get ahead' first become true for my family?") points the writing at a specific memory, story, or sentence. The specificity gets past the rehearsed answer.

Rebecca's 84 prompts across The Money Journal are tight in this way. The library reuses many of them directly; new prompts in the library follow the same template — one specific question, naming a specific feeling, story, or person.
What to do with what surfaces

Most of what comes up in a free-write doesn't need anything done with it. The writing is the practice. Some prompts surface a line that catches — a name, a phrase, a memory that lands sharper than the rest. That line is worth coming back to: open the notebook to that page next week and write for five more minutes on that one line.

Prompts paired with tapping (which the library does occasionally) often surface the line that the tapping then works with. The set-up statement of the tapping round names what surfaced; the rounds release it.

A notebook and a pen. A timer (any phone). Somewhere you won't be interrupted for fifteen minutes. Most users keep one notebook for Mindset journal work; some keep separate notebooks per category (money, body, relationships). Both work.

The tight-question, timed, no-editing free-write is the method Rebecca J Page uses across The Money Journal: 12 Weeks to Peace, Freedom & Overflow (2025), which holds 84 daily prompts, 12 weekly reflections, and 12 weekly rituals. The wider lineage of free-writing as a practice has multiple sources; Julia Cameron's morning pages in The Artist's Way (1992) and Peter Elbow's freewriting (Writing Without Teachers, 1973) are the most prominent in English-language writing instruction. Rebecca's version is tighter than either (five minutes against a timer, on a single specific question), and that tightness is what makes it daily-life-sized.
